title: Meeting Rooms II
description:
## Meeting Rooms II

Given an array of meeting time intervals where `intervals[i] = [start_i, end_i]`, return the minimum number of conference rooms required to hold all meetings simultaneously.

**Constraints:**
- `1 <= intervals.length <= 10^4`
- `0 <= start_i < end_i <= 10^6`

examples.explanation: [2,4] ends before [7,10] starts — one room suffices.

hiddenCases.explanation: One long meeting plus shorter concurrent ones

solutions.label: Min-Heap — O(n log n)
solutions.explanation: Sort meetings by start time. Use a min-heap to track when each occupied room becomes free (storing end times). For each new meeting, check if the earliest-ending room is free by start time — if so, reuse it (pop and push new end time). Otherwise, open a new room (just push). The heap size at the end equals the number of rooms needed. This works because we always reuse the room that frees up soonest.

solutions.label: Chronological Events — O(n log n)
solutions.code:
from typing import List

def min_meeting_rooms(intervals: List[List[int]]) -> int:
    starts = sorted(s for s, e in intervals)
    ends = sorted(e for s, e in intervals)

    rooms = 0
    max_rooms = 0
    i, j = 0, 0

    while i < len(starts):
        if starts[i] < ends[j]:
            rooms += 1
            max_rooms = max(max_rooms, rooms)
            i += 1
        else:
            rooms -= 1
            j += 1

    return max_rooms
solutions.explanation: Sort start times and end times separately. Use two pointers to merge these sorted events chronologically. When a start event is processed before the next end event, a new room is needed (rooms++). When an end event comes first, a room is freed (rooms--). Track the maximum concurrent rooms. This is equivalent to finding the maximum overlap at any point in time.

hints: Sort by start time. Use a min-heap to track when each room becomes free.
For each new meeting, if the earliest-ending room frees up before this meeting starts, reuse it. Otherwise, open a new room.
